does: This paper introduces JailFuzzer, a fuzzing-based framework that leverages LLM agents to automatically generate natural, semantically meaningful jailbreak prompts for text-to-image generative models. The approach adapts classical fuzzing methodology from software testing by implementing three core components: a seed pool containing initial prompts and known jailbreaks, a guided mutation engine powered by LLMs that creates variations while preserving semantic meaning and naturalness, and an oracle function to detect successful jailbreaks. The framework operates in a black-box setting without requiring access to model internals or gradients, addressing practical deployment scenarios. JailFuzzer generates significantly fewer queries than existing methods while producing prompts that are harder to detect automatically compared to prior work, demonstrating efficient exploration of the jailbreak space.
connects: This work builds on decades of fuzzing methodology from software security, applying these principles to AI safety by treating jailbreak generation as a search problem over prompt space rather than code space. It extends recent research on adversarial attacks against generative models and jailbreaking methods that either require white-box access or produce easily detectable unnatural prompts. The paper responds to limitations in prior work like prompt injection techniques and genetic algorithm-based attacks that suffer from high computational overhead or restricted search spaces. It also incorporates advances in using LLMs as reasoning engines for complex tasks, recognizing that LLMs themselves can guide the generation of adversarial prompts through intelligent mutation rather than random perturbation. The combination of classical fuzzing with modern language model capabilities represents a novel cross-disciplinary approach.
enables: This framework opens several research directions in adversarial robustness testing for generative models. It provides an efficient black-box evaluation methodology that security teams and researchers can deploy to assess vulnerability of T2I systems without model access, informing the design of better safety mechanisms. The naturalness and semantic coherence of generated jailbreaks could enable studies on why current safety filters fail against meaningful variations of harmful requests. Follow-up work could extend the fuzzing approach to other modalities (vision-language models, audio generation) and explore defensive mechanisms specifically designed to resist fuzzing-driven attacks. The framework also raises questions about the trade-offs between semantic naturalness and safety, potentially informing development of more robust alignment techniques for generative models.
